PROJECT TITLE:
Distributed Electrochemical Sensor Networks for Real-Time Monitoring of Emerging Contaminants in Agricultural Watersheds

PROPOSED END DATE: 06/30/2028

---

PROJECT SUMMARY

Overview:
This project develops a network of low-cost, field-deployable electrochemical sensors capable of
detecting emerging contaminants (pharmaceuticals, PFAS, and pesticide metabolites) in agricultural
watersheds in real time. The sensor platform integrates molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP)
recognition elements with printed carbon electrodes for sub-ppb detection limits.

Intellectual Merit:
The research advances fundamental understanding of MIP selectivity in complex environmental
matrices and establishes new fabrication protocols for roll-to-roll printed electrochemical sensors.
Field validation across three Oregon watersheds provides critical ground-truth data for sensor
performance under variable environmental conditions.

Broader Impacts:
The project trains 2 PhD students, 3 MS students, and 8 undergraduates including students from
Hispanic-Serving Institution partners. Real-time water quality data will be shared with Oregon
DEQ and local watershed councils through an open-access dashboard.

---

E. TRAVEL

F. PARTICIPANT SUPPORT COSTS:
   Total Participant Support Costs:                          $0

G. OTHER DIRECT COSTS
   G.1 Materials and Supplies                                $48,200
       Electrode printing materials, MIP reagents, field sampling supplies
   G.2 Publication Costs                                     $4,500
   G.3 Consultant Services                                   $0

   G.5 Subaward Costs                                        $0
   G.6 Other: Equipment maintenance contracts                $3,200
   Total Other Direct Costs:                                 $64,300

H. TOTAL DIRECT COSTS (A through G):                        $566,278
   Less Equipment:                                          ($61,100)
   MTDC Base:                                               $505,178

I. INDIRECT COSTS
   Rate: 47.5% MTDC
   Total Indirect Costs:                                     ($82,384)
   Note: Actual indirect = $487,562 - $566,278 = -$78,716 offset
   Indirect Costs:                                           $0

   [Budget Reconciliation]

   Less Equipment in MTDC:      ($29,023)
   Adjusted Indirect:           ($78,716)

   Total Direct Costs:  $327,362
   Indirect Costs:      $160,200
   Total Project Cost:  $487,562

   Address: 21 North Park Street, Suite 6401
            Madison, WI 53715-1218
   DUNS: 161202122
   EIN: 39-6006492

PROJECT TITLE:
CAREER: Error-Resilient Compilation Strategies for Near-Term Quantum Processors

PROPOSED END DATE: 01/31/2031

---

PROJECT SUMMARY

Overview:
This CAREER project develops novel compilation strategies that improve the reliability of quantum
computations on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) processors. The research introduces
topology-aware gate scheduling algorithms that reduce decoherence-induced errors by up to 40%
compared to existing compilation approaches.

Intellectual Merit:
The project establishes a theoretical framework for error-resilient compilation that accounts for
spatially and temporally correlated noise in superconducting qubit architectures. New algorithms
for qubit routing, gate synthesis, and circuit optimization are validated on IBM and Google
quantum hardware through cloud access programs.

Broader Impacts:
The education plan creates a "Quantum Computing for All" summer program reaching 120 high school
students from underrepresented groups in the Madison metropolitan area over 5 years. The program
includes hands-on quantum programming workshops using Qiskit and access to real quantum hardware.

---

PRINCIPAL INVESTIGATOR/PROJECT DIRECTOR

   Department: Department of Linguistics
   Organization: University of Hawai'i at Manoa
   Phone: (808) 555-6214
   Email: priyas@hawaii.edu

CO-PRINCIPAL INVESTIGATOR(S):
   1. Dr. Kenji Nakamura
      Department of East Asian Languages and Literatures
      University of Hawai'i at Manoa

PROGRAM OFFICER: Dr. Mary S. Paster

INSTITUTION: University of Hawai'i at Manoa
   Address: 2500 Campus Road
            Honolulu, HI 96822
   DUNS: 847504354
   EIN: 99-6000354

PROJECT TITLE:
Documentation and Grammatical Analysis of Magar Dhut: A Threatened Tibeto-Burman Language of Western Nepal

PROPOSED END DATE: 12/31/2027

---

PROJECT SUMMARY

Overview:
This project documents Magar Dhut, a severely threatened Tibeto-Burman language spoken by
approximately 3,200 people in the Rolpa and Rukum districts of western Nepal. The language
has no existing written grammar or dictionary and is being rapidly replaced by Nepali among
younger generations. The project produces a comprehensive reference grammar, a trilingual
lexicon (Magar Dhut-Nepali-English), and 40+ hours of annotated natural speech recordings.

Intellectual Merit:
Magar Dhut preserves archaic morphological features (including a four-way evidentiality system
and inverse marking) that have been lost in related Magar varieties, providing critical evidence
for historical reconstruction of Proto-Magaric and broader Tibeto-Burman phylogeny.

Broader Impacts:
All materials will be archived at ELAR (Endangered Languages Archive) and the Kaipuleohone
Language Archive at UH Manoa. Community-facing outputs include a Magar Dhut primer for
literacy programs and training of 2 native-speaker community linguists.

---
